items.q: Can the orders in a rate equation be read from the coefficients of the balanced equation?
items.correct: No — orders come only from experimental data; the stoichiometric coefficients tell you nothing about the rate equation.
items.misconception: The order with respect to each reactant equals its coefficient in the balanced equation.
items.random_wrong: The overall order always equals the number of products formed.
items.why: Students assume the balanced equation describes the mechanism, but the rate equation reflects only the steps up to the rate-determining step.

items.q: Does increasing a reactant's concentration change the rate constant k?
items.correct: No — k depends only on temperature (and the presence of a catalyst); changing concentration changes the rate, not k.
items.misconception: Increasing concentration increases k because the reaction goes faster.
items.random_wrong: k decreases when more reactant is added because the reactant gets used up.
items.why: Students treat k as just another name for rate, instead of the temperature-dependent constant that links rate to concentration.

items.q: If a reactant has order zero, does it still take part in the reaction?
items.correct: Yes, it can — zero order means it is consumed in a step after the rate-determining step, so its concentration does not affect the rate.
items.misconception: A zero-order reactant is not actually used up in the reaction.
items.random_wrong: Zero order means doubling that reactant doubles the rate.
items.why: Students equate 'no effect on rate' with 'no role in the reaction', missing the link between orders and the mechanism's slow step.

items.q: Doubling [A] doubles the rate; doubling [B] has no effect. The rate equation is:
items.why: Correct answer: rate = k[A]. “rate = k[A][B]” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The units of k for a reaction that is second order overall (rate in mol dm⁻³ s⁻¹) are:
items.why: Correct answer: mol⁻¹ dm³ s⁻¹. “s⁻¹” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which change increases the value of the rate constant k?
items.misconception: Increasing the concentration of a reactant
items.random_wrong: Grinding a solid reactant more finely
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Raising the temperature. “Increasing the concentration of a reactant” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A reaction is zero order with respect to B. Doubling [B]:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Leaves the rate unchanged. “Doubles the rate” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: For rate = k[A]², the rate is 1.6 × 10⁻³ mol dm⁻³ s⁻¹ when [A] = 0.20 mol dm⁻³. Calculate k in mol⁻¹ dm³ s⁻¹.
items.why: Exact answer: 0.04 mol⁻¹ dm³ s⁻¹. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
